mnemonics.title: Cannot Overload These
mnemonics.content: CANT: Conditional (?:), Array [], New/Delete, Template, sizeof, ::, Dot operator, Member pointer (.*), typeid - These cannot be overloaded!

mnemonics.title: Operator Rules Remember
mnemonics.content: RULES: arity (unary/binary) cannot change, Precedence stays same, At least one user-defined operand, Can't overload for only primitives, Friends for stream ops (<< >>)

acronyms.expansion: Object-Oriented Programming with C++ - Subject code BCS306B

acronyms.usage: Operator overloading cannot change arity of operators

acronyms.expansion: Destructor, Copy Constructor, Copy Assignment Operator
acronyms.usage: When managing dynamic memory, define all three for proper resource management

visualTips.visualization: Think of prefix as placing ++ before variable: ++a (increment first, use second). Postfix: a++ (use first, increment second). Draw timeline showing order of operations.
Draw circle for class. Member functions are inside (use 'this'), Friends are outside but can peek inside (access private). Left operand is implicit 'this' for member.
Chain: a=b=c. Show how reference returns 'this' allowing c->b->a flow. Value returns a COPY (new object), breaking the chain.

analogies.analogy: Like a multilingual person who speaks differently to different people but means the same thing. Operators behave differently for different data types.
Like a currency exchange booth. A class object gets 'exchanged' into another type (int, double, etc.) automatically.

analogies.analization: Like a bookshelf where you can directly access any book by position number. operator[] makes your object act like an array.

analogies.analogy: A functor is like a named function that also carries data (like a person with a notepad who can do a task).
